pattern: Using more of the queries in order only ever adds decrement capacity, never removes it, so feasibility is monotone in how many queries you've applied -- which turns 'find the smallest working prefix' into a binary search over a coverage check.
hints: If using the first k queries can zero the array, using the first k+1 can too, since you gain strictly more decrementing power -- so feasibility as a function of k only ever switches on once.

For a fixed k, the maximum possible decrement at an index is the sum of the val of every one of the first k queries whose range covers it -- a weighted difference array gives you that sum in one pass.
Binary search on k: the answer is the smallest k whose weighted coverage is at least nums[i] everywhere, or -1 if even every query together cannot reach that.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, two of three queries suffice
statement example, impossible even with all queries
already all zero, zero queries needed
single element, single query, exact val
first query alone insufficient, need both
later queries would help but earlier prefix already suffices

stress.description: random small values with random weighted ranges
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 1500))
    nums = [rng.randint(0, 8) for _ in range(n)]
    m = rng.randint(1, 150)
    queries = []
    for _ in range(m):
        l = rng.randint(0, n - 1)
        r = rng.randint(l, n - 1)
        val = rng.randint(1, 5)
        queries.append([l, r, val])
    return [nums, queries]
